This matter remained unresolved at the time of finalisation of the audit report. (iii)  IRPL holds 60% equity shares in a subsidiary, AutoBots Ltd. The statutory auditors of AutoBots Ltd., in their audit report for FY 2024–25, expressed a qualified opinion due to non-provision of doubtful debts amounting to ` 1.5 crores, which the auditor considered materially misstated. During the final phase of the audit, on 15th April 2025, IRPL's CEO offered a position of 'Head of Software Development' to CA. Gaurav's wife, a highly qualified software engineer. CA. Gaurav subsequently discovered that his father purchased 1,00,000 equity shares of ` 10 each in AutoBots Ltd., a subsidiary of IRPL on 27th June, 2025 i.e., after issuance of audit report. The total paid-up capital of AutoBots Ltd. is ` 5 crores. In the final meeting on 25th April 2025, IRPL's management strongly contended that the unresolved matters were minor in the context of their overall profitable operations. They urged CA. Gaurav to issue an unmodified audit opinion, warning that any modification could jeopardize a crucial funding deal for the company.
Question_Text: What is the reporting responsibility of CA. Gaurav under para 3(i) of CARO 2020, regarding the undisposed robotic arm with a written down value of ` 2.5 crores that was not physically located?
Option_A: Since the management has provided a verbal explanation that the asset is in transit for calibration, CA. Gaurav should accept it and is not required to report any discrepancy in the CARO report.
Option_B: The discrepancy is not considered material as it represents only a small portion of the company's total turnover of ` 120 crores, and therefore, no reporting under CARO 2020 is necessary.
Option_C: CA. Gaurav should state in his CARO report that a material discrepancy of ` 2.5 crores was noticed during the physical verification of Property, Plant, and Equipment, and this has not been properly dealt with in the books of account.
Option_D: CA. Gaurav should qualify his main audit report for this matter but can choose not to mention this specific discrepancy in the CARO report to avoid redundancy.
Reasoning: Companies (Auditor's Report) Order, 2020, requires the auditor to state whether any material discrepancies were noticed on physical verification of Property, Plant, and Equipment and if so, whether they have been properly dealt with in the books of account. A fixed asset with a WDV of ₹ 2.5 crores being physically untraceable without any supporting documentation (like a gate pass, challan, or insurance) constitutes a material discrepancy. Management's verbal explanation is insufficient audit evidence. Therefore, CA Gaurav must report this material discrepancy and the fact that it has not been properly accounted for.

Scenario_Text: Innovate Robotics Pvt. Ltd. (IRPL) engaged in the business of manufacturing advanced robotic machinery and developing proprietary automation software appointed M/s Gaur & Associates, Chartered Accountants, as the statutory auditor for the financial year 2024-25. The financial highlights of IRPL for the year were as follows: Particulars Balance as at 31st March 2024) Total Turnover (Previous Year: ` 85 crores) ` 120 crores Borrowings from banks and financial institutions ` 35 crores Paid-up Share Capital ` 20 crores Profit Before Tax ` 15 crores During the audit, Engagement Partner CA. Gaurav and his team observed the various issues which are given as under: (i) While conducting substantive procedures, the audit team noticed a significant increase in sales during the last quarter of the financial year. A major portion of this increase, amounting to ` 8 crores, was attributed to sales made to a new customer, TechSynergy Solutions Pvt. Ltd., which was incorporated on 1st October, 2024. The audit team’s inquiries revealed that the payment for these sales was received on 5th April, 2025, just before the audit commenced. Upon further investigation, Anjali, an audit assistant, discovered that one of the directors of TechSynergy Solutions was the husband of the daughter of IRPL's Chief Financial Officer (CFO), Mr. Alok. When CA. Gaurav brought this information to Mr. Alok’s attention, the CFO dismissed it as a mere coincidence and insisted that the transaction was conducted at arm’s length. He also argued that since the payment was fully received, there was no risk of a misstatement. Management also refused to disclose this relationship in the financial statements, contending that it was not a related party transaction as per the applicable financial reporting framework, and that doing so would unnecessarily complicate matters. It was also observed that approval by way of resolution was also not obtained by management before entering into the contract with TechSynergy for sale of goods. (ii) During the physical verification of Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE), the audit team & management could not locate a highly specialised robotic arm, which was recorded in the fixed asset register with a written down value of ` 2.5 crores. Later, management provided a verbal explanation that the asset had been sent to a facility in Germany for immediate calibration in February 2025 and was in transit. However, they failed to provide any supporting documentation such as gate pass, dispatch challan, or insurance documentation for this movement. This matter remained unresolved at the time of finalisation of the audit report. (iii)  IRPL holds 60% equity shares in a subsidiary, AutoBots Ltd. The statutory auditors of AutoBots Ltd., in their audit report for FY 2024–25, expressed a qualified opinion due to non-provision of doubtful debts amounting to ` 1.5 crores, which the auditor considered materially misstated. During the final phase of the audit, on 15th April 2025, IRPL's CEO offered a position of 'Head of Software Development' to CA. Gaurav's wife, a highly qualified software engineer. CA. Gaurav subsequently discovered that his father purchased 1,00,000 equity shares of ` 10 each in AutoBots Ltd., a subsidiary of IRPL on 27th June, 2025 i.e., after issuance of audit report. The total paid-up capital of AutoBots Ltd. is ` 5 crores. In the final meeting on 25th April 2025, IRPL's management strongly contended that the unresolved matters were minor in the context of their overall profitable operations. They urged CA. Gaurav to issue an unmodified audit opinion, warning that any modification could jeopardize a crucial funding deal for the company.
Question_Text: Assuming that CA. Gaurav's father purchased the shares with a face value of ` 10 lakhs in AutoBots Ltd., the subsidiary company, on 1st April, 2025, what is the implication on the audit engagement of IRPL under the Companies Act, 2013 and the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949?
Option_A: There is no implication, as the shares are held in the subsidiary company (AutoBots Ltd.) and not in the holding company (IRPL).
Option_B: CA. Gaurav is disqualified from being the auditor of IRPL because his relative holds securities in a subsidiary of the company exceeding the prescribed limit of ` 1 lakh, and continuing the engagement constitutes professional misconduct.
Option_C: Since CA. Gaurav was not aware of his father's investment at the time of accepting the audit, he is not in violation, provided he now obtains a declaration from his father confirming it is not a material investment.
Option_D: CA. Gaurav is disqualified from being the auditor of IRPL because his relative holds securities in a subsidiary of the company exceeding the prescribed limit of ` 1 lakh, but the disqualification can be retrospectively corrected if his father sells the shares within 60 days of CA. Gaurav becoming aware of the holding, thereby avoiding any misconduct.
Reasoning: As per Section 141(3)(d)(i) of the Companies Act, 2013, an auditor is disqualified if he or his relative holds any security or interest in the company, its subsidiary, its holding company, or its associate company. The proviso to this section allows a relative to hold securities or interest in the company of face value not exceeding ₹1,00,000. In this case, CA Gaurav's father is his relative. He holds shares in AutoBots Ltd., a subsidiary of IRPL. The face value of these shares is ₹10,00,000 (1,00,000 shares x ₹10), which is significantly higher than the permitted threshold of ₹1,00,000. Therefore, CA Gaurav is disqualified from being appointed as the auditor of IRPL. Continuing to act as an auditor while being disqualified contravenes the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013, and is deemed professional misconduct under Clause (9) of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949.

Question_Text: What is the most appropriate type of audit opinion that CA. Gaurav should express for IRPL's financial statements in accordance with SA 700 regarding multiple unresolved issues, specifically the ` 8 crore revenue transaction, the missing ` 2.5 crore asset and the impact of the subsidiary's qualified opinion?
Option_A: An adverse opinion, because the collective impact of the identified misstatements is so material and pervasive that the financial statements as a whole are misleading.
Option_B: A qualified opinion, as the issues identified are material but are confined to specific elements of the financial statements and are not pervasive.
Option_C: A disclaimer of opinion, because the inability to physically verify a key asset constitutes a significant limitation on the scope of the audit.
Option_D: An unmodified opinion with a Key Audit Matters (KAM) paragraph describing the revenue recognition and asset verification issues as high-risk areas.
Reasoning: An adverse opinion is appropriate in this scenario. According to SA 705 (Revised), an auditor should express an adverse opinion when, having obtained sufficient appropriate audit evidence, they conclude that misstatements, individually or in the aggregate, are both material and pervasive to the financial statements. The situation involves: 1. A potential fraudulent revenue transaction of ₹8 crores, where management refuses disclosure, casting doubt on their integrity. This misstatement alone is highly material relative to the Profit Before Tax of ₹15 crores. 2. An unverified asset of ₹2.5 crores with no supporting documentation. 3. The effect of a qualified opinion in a subsidiary. The combination of these issues, particularly the one related to revenue which suggests management override and potential fraud, is not isolated. It indicates a pervasive lack of reliability in the financial statements. Therefore, the financial statements as a whole are materially misstated and misleading.

Question_Text: If CA. Gaurav surrenders to the pressure from IRPL's management and issues an unmodified audit report for the year ended 31st March 2025, despite the unresolved issues regarding the revenue transaction with TechSynergy and the unverified robotic arm, he would be guilty of professional misconduct. Which of the following clauses of Part I of the Second Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949, would be applicable?
Option_A: Clause 5 and 9
Option_B: Clause 6 and 4
Option_C: Clause 5 and 8
Option_D: Clause 5, 6 and 7
Reasoning: The correct answer is (d) because issuing an unmodified opinion under these circumstances would violate all three clauses mentioned. • Clause (5) is applicable because CA Gaurav is aware of the relationship between IRPL's CFO and a director of TechSynergy Solutions. This is a material fact that management has refused to disclose. Failure to ensure this disclosure makes the financial statements misleading, constituting misconduct under Clause (5). • Clause (6) is applicable because CA Gaurav has identified two clear material misstatements: the potential fraudulent revenue of ₹8 crores and the unaccounted-for asset of ₹2.5 crores. Knowingly failing to report these misstatements in his audit report is a direct violation of Clause (6). • Clause (7) is applicable because ignoring such significant audit evidence, resulting to management pressure, and failing to adhere to the fundamental principles of auditing (like obtaining sufficient appropriate evidence and maintaining professional skepticism) would be considered a case of gross negligence and a failure to exercise due diligence.

Scenario_Text: SCL is a prominent, listed Non-Banking Financial Company (NBFC) (non-deposit taking NBFCs with an asset size of ` 1,000 crore and above) primarily engaged in providing corporate loans and retail financing. Mehta & Shah Associates (MSA), a partnership firm with two partners, CA. Karan Mehta and CA. Arjun Shah, was appointed as the statutory auditor of SwiftFin Capital Ltd. (SCL) for the financial year ended 31st March, 2025. During the audit, MSA team led by CA. Mehta, began reviewing SCL's compliance with RBI's Master Directions for NBFCs. They discovered that three large corporate loan accounts, with a total outstanding balance of ` 15 crores, had principal and/or interest payments overdue for a period ranging from 95 to 110 days as at the balance sheet date. However, these accounts were still classified as 'Standard Assets' in the company's records. The management of SCL argued that these were long-standing, "blue-chip" clients with whom they were in advanced restructuring negotiations. They contended that classifying these accounts as Non-Performing Assets (NPAs) would trigger cross-default clauses in other agreements of those clients and permanently damage valuable business relationships. They insisted that the non-classification was a temporary measure based on commercial judgment. While reviewing the board minutes, the audit team came to know about a lawsuit filed against SCL in January 2025. A group of retail customers alleged that the company had provided loans by charging interest rates that were not transparently disclosed and were in violation of the RBI's Fair Practices Code. SCL's internal legal department had prepared a note for the board estimating a financial outcome of up to ` 5 crores if the judgment went against the company. However, the financial statements contained no provision or disclosure for this matter. Management asserted that their legal advisors believed the probability of an unfavourable outcome was "remote". In April 2025, impressed with the firm's diligence, the management of SCL also offered the tax audit engagement under Section 44AB of the Income-tax Act, 1961, to MSA for the Assessment Year 2025-26. CA. Mehta reviewed the firm's workload for the assessment year. As on that date, CA. Mehta had already signed 55 tax audit reports, and his partner, CA. Arjun, had signed 60 tax audit reports.
Question_Text: Considering the requirements of SA 250, "Consideration of Laws and Regulations in an Audit of Financial Statements" and the specific context of an NBFC audit, what is the most appropriate course of action for CA. Mehta regarding the non-classification of loan accounts worth ` 15 crores as NPAs?
Option_A: CA. Mehta should accept management's commercial judgment, as advanced restructuring negotiations are in progress, and disclose the matter in an "Emphasis of Matter" paragraph to ensure user awareness.
Option_B: CA. Mehta should inform the management that this non-classification constitutes a material misstatement due to non-compliance with RBI's mandatory prudential norms. If management refuses to correct this by reclassifying the assets and making provisions, he must modify the audit opinion.
Option_C: CA. Mehta's primary responsibility is to report this specific instance of non-compliance with IRAC norms to the Department of Supervision of the RBI, and he can issue an unmodified opinion on the financial statements.
Option_D: CA. Mehta should obtain a specific written representation from management confirming the future recoverability of the loans and based on this representation, he can agree to the existing classification and issue a clean report.
Reasoning: The RBI's Prudential Norms on Income Recognition, Asset Classification, and Provisioning (IRAC norms) are laws and regulations that have a direct effect on the determination of material amounts and disclosures in an NBFC's financial statements. According to SA 250 “Consideration of Laws and Regulations in an Audit of Financial Statements”, the auditor has a responsibility to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence regarding compliance with such laws and regulations. The overdue status of over 90 days requires a mandatory classification as NPA. Management's "commercial judgment" cannot override these specific regulatory requirements. This non-classification results in a material misstatement of assets and profits. Therefore, CA Mehta must request management to correct the financials. If they refuse, he is obligated under SA 705 (Revised) to issue a modified opinion (qualified or adverse, depending on pervasiveness).

In April 2025, impressed with the firm's diligence, the management of SCL also offered the tax audit engagement under Section 44AB of the Income-tax Act, 1961, to MSA for the Assessment Year 2025-26. Question_Text: With reference to the tax audit engagement offered by SCL and assuming that the CA. Arjun is finalised as the signing on behalf of the firm Mehta & Shah Associates (MSA), can the firm legally accept this new Tax Audit assignment for the Assessment Year 2025-26 as per the guidelines issued by the Council of ICAI?
Option_A: No, the firm cannot accept the engagement because the total number of tax audits conducted by the partner CA. Arjun (60) is very close to the limit, and accepting one more would be a violation.
Option_B: Yes, the firm can accept the engagement, but the audit report must be signed by CA. Mehta, as CA. Arjun has already reached his permissible limit of 60 audits.
Option_C: No, the firm cannot accept the engagement because the specified limit of 60 tax audits applies to the firm as a whole, and the partners' individual counts are irrelevant.
Option_D: Yes, the firm can accept the engagement because the specified limit of 60 tax audits applies to each partner individually, and the firm can still take five more Tax Audits before reaching its 120 Tax Audit limit.
Reasoning: According to the Council General Guidelines, 2008 (Chapter VI), a Chartered Accountant in practice is permitted to conduct a maximum of 60 tax audits under Section 44AB of the Income-tax Act, 1961, per assessment year. This limit is applicable per partner in a firm. Paragraph 6.1.7 of Chapter VI of Council General Guidelines, 2008, clarifies that the limit on the number of tax audit assignments per partner in a CA Firm may be distributed between the partners in any manner whatsoever. In the case of MSA, which has two partners, the firm can collectively handle up to 120 tax audits (60 for CA Mehta + 60 for CA Shah). As of the offer date, CA Mehta has signed 55 and CA Shah has signed 60 reports. Therefore, the firm can legally accept the new tax audit, and it can be signed by either of the partners.

Question_Text: CA. Mehta has identified the matter regarding NPA classification and the non-disclosure of the lawsuit as the two most significant issues of the entire audit. He wishes to highlight these matters prominently in his report as Key Audit Matters. Considering the nature of matters, which of the following is the only appropriate method for CA. Mehta to communicate these in his audit report?
Option_A: Describe both issues in detail in the "Key Audit Matters" (KAM) section, as they represent the areas of highest risk and significant auditor judgment.
Option_B: Describe both issues in an "Emphasis of Matter" (EOM) paragraph, as they are fundamental to understanding the company's financial position and risks.
Option_C: Describe the issues giving rise to the misstatements in the "Basis for Modification" paragraph, which will support a qualified or adverse opinion.
Option_D: Describe the issues in an "Other Matter" (OM) paragraph, as they are relevant to the users' understanding of the conduct of the audit and management's integrity.
Reasoning: Since there are identified material misstatements that management has refused to correct, this is the only appropriate placement for these matters.

Question_Text: Apart from making inquiries with management wherein the management’s assessment of the lawsuit is "remote," which audit procedures should be designed and performed by CA. Mehta?
Option_A: He should discuss the matter with audit committee, expressing concern that management's representation is unreliable and insists on making a provision of ` 5 crores required in the financial statements based on the available evidence and his independent assessment.
Option_B: He should seek direct communication with the entity’s external legal counsel through a letter of inquiry, prepared and sent by management, requesting the entity’s external legal counsel to provide a necessary update on the litigation.
Option_C: He should seek direct communication with the entity’s external legal counsel through a letter of inquiry, prepared by management and sent by the auditor, requesting the entity’s external legal counsel to communicate directly with the auditor.
Option_D: After seeking direct communication with the entity’s external legal, he should independently engage a forensic accountant at the firm's own cost to obtain the evidence against the lawsuit, which should be admissible in the court of law.
Reasoning: If the auditor assesses a risk of material misstatement regarding litigation or claims that have been identified, or when audit procedures performed indicate that other material litigation or claims may exist, the auditor shall, in addition to the procedures required by other SAs, seek direct communication with the entity’s external legal counsel. The auditor shall do so through a letter of inquiry, prepared by management and sent by the auditor, requesting the entity’s external legal counsel to communicate directly with the auditor.

Scenario_Text: Prakriti Limited is a listed company engaged in the manufacture of eco-friendly home appliances. During the financial year, the company significantly expanded its operations by setting up a new manufacturing facility in a semi-rural location. The expansion was aimed at increasing production capacity while benefiting from lower operational costs and proximity to raw material suppliers. During the statutory audit of Prakriti Limited, the auditor became aware of the undisclosed environmental violations and labour-related non-compliances. Management represented that the issues were operational in nature and assured corrective action. Relying on management representations, the auditor did not include any specific reference to these matters in the audit report. The company sources raw materials from a diverse supplier base and employs a large workforce, a substantial portion of which consists of contractual labour engaged through third-party contractors. Prakriti Limited sells its products through online platforms as well as a widespread network of distributors across India. Being a listed entity, Prakriti Limited is required to comply with the Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework. During the year, the following issues came to the attention of stakeholders and regulators: • Prakriti Limited has published its sustainability policies and environmental commitments on its official website. However, certain material environmental violations relating to emissions and waste management at the newly established plant were disclosed to the public only after these violations were highlighted in media reports. The company did not make timely or voluntary disclosures to its stakeholders when the violations first occurred. • Contractual workers employed at the new manufacturing facility raised complaints regarding delayed payment of wages by contractors and absence of adequate safety measures at the workplace. The workers alleged that despite being aware of these issues, the company did not take sufficient steps to ensure compliance by contractors. • Prakriti Limited collects customer data through its online sales platform for order processing and customer engagement. During the year, it was observed that the company shared customer data with third-party marketing agencies without obtaining explicit consent from customers. Statutory auditor CA. Manik, during the statutory audit of Prakriti Limited, became aware of the undisclosed environmental violations and labour-related non-compliances. Management represented that the issues were operational in nature and assured corrective action. Relying on management representations, the auditor did not include any specific reference to these matters in the audit report.
Question_Text: The delayed disclosure of environmental violations at the new manufacturing plant is contrary to which BRSR principle?
Option_A: Principle 2 – Safe and Sustainable Goods and Services
Option_B: Principle 1 – Ethics, Transparency and Accountability
Option_C: Principle 6 – Protection and Restoration of Environment
Option_D: Principle 9 – Provide Value to the Consumers in a Responsible Manner
Reasoning: Principle 1 requires businesses to conduct operations with integrity and transparency and to ensure timely, accurate and complete disclosure of material information to stakeholders. Delayed disclosure of environmental violations, especially after media intervention, reflects a lack of transparency and accountability, thereby violating Principle 1.

Question_Text: Which clause of CARO, 2020 is most likely to require reporting in respect of delayed wages and labour-related statutory non-compliances at Prakriti Limited?
Option_A: Clause 3(vii) – Statutory Dues
Option_B: Clause 3(i) – Property, Plant and Equipment;
Option_C: Clause 3(xi) – Fraud by the company
Option_D: Clause 3(xv) – Non-cash transactions with directors
Reasoning: Clause (vii) of Paragraph 3 of CARO, 2020 requires reporting on whether the company is regular in depositing undisputed statutory dues including Goods and Services Tax, provident fund, employees' state insurance, income-tax, sales-tax, service tax, duty of customs, duty of excise, value added tax, cess and any other statutory dues to the appropriate authorities and if not, the extent of the arrears of outstanding statutory dues as on the last day of the financial year concerned for a period of more than six months from the date they became payable, shall be indicated.

Question_Text: The grievances raised by contractual workers regarding delayed wages and unsafe working conditions primarily indicate non-compliance with which principle?
Option_A: Principle 4 – Respect for Stakeholders’ Interests and Responsiveness
Option_B: Principle 2 – Safe and Sustainable Goods and Services
Option_C: Principle 8 – Promote Inclusive Growth and Equitable Development
Option_D: Principle 3 – Promote Well-Being of All Employees Including those in the Value Chain
Reasoning: Principle 3 extends beyond permanent employees and includes workers in the value chain, such as contractual labour. Timely payment of wages and provision of safe working conditions are core elements of employee well-being. Failure to address wage delays and workplace safety concerns indicates non-compliance with this principle.

Question_Text: Which is the most appropriate response of the auditor upon becoming aware of environmental and labour law non-compliances as per relevant Standards on Auditing?
Option_A: Accept management representation as sufficient audit evidence
Option_B: Ignore the matters since they relate to non-financial reporting
Option_C: Evaluate implications under applicable laws and communicate with those charged with governance, and consider appropriate reporting
Option_D: Immediately resign from the audit
Reasoning: As per SA 250, the auditor must consider non-compliance with laws and regulations and communicate with those charged with governance. Further, reliance solely on management representation by the auditor violates SA 580 and the principle of professional skepticism.

Question_Text: Sharing customer data with third-party marketing agencies without explicit consent amounts to a violation of:
Option_A: Principle 2 – Safe and Sustainable Goods and Services:

Scenario_Text: Wire Ltd. is a listed company engaged in the manufacture of electrical equipment. The company operates multiple manufacturing units across India and consumes significant quantities of electricity, water, and natural resources such as copper and aluminium for its operations. The production process results in carbon emissions, water discharge, hazardous waste, and e-waste. To address environmental concerns, Wire Ltd. has installed solar power facilities at its plants and implemented water recycling systems. The company has also set measurable targets to reduce its carbon emissions by 30% over the next five years and periodically tracks its emission levels. Wire Ltd. employs a large workforce, including permanent and contract workers. The company has framed policies relating to employee health and safety, labour welfare, gender diversity, equal opportunity, and skill development. It also undertakes community development initiatives in the areas surrounding its manufacturing units. The Board of Wire Ltd. comprises executive and independent directors. The company has constituted various Board committees, including the Audit Committee, Nomination and Remuneration Committee, and Risk Management Committee. Wire Ltd. follows a code of conduct, has a whistle-blower mechanism, and ensures compliance with applicable laws and regulations. In response to increasing investor and regulatory expectations, Wire Ltd. has initiated Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) reporting, which includes both qualitative disclosures (such as policies and strategies) and quantitative disclosures (such as metrics and key performance indicators). Wire Ltd. appoints a firm of Chartered Accountants APR & Associates to provide ESG assurance using advanced digital auditing tools such as data analytics and automated extraction of ESG data from its ERP systems. Subsequently, CA. Ankur, Partner APR & Associates instruct his article assistant CA Mihir to publish a detailed post on its website and social media platforms highlighting its role in conducting “ESG and Digital Audits of a listed manufacturing company using AI-based tools” and to invites other corporates to approach the firm for similar assignments.
Question_Text: Which of the following activities undertaken by Wire Ltd. falls under the Environmental (E) pillar of ESG?
Option_A: Constitution of Board-level committees
Option_B: Adoption of a whistle-blower mechanism
Option_C: Installation of solar power facilities and water recycling systems
Option_D: Community development initiatives
Reasoning: The environmental pillar include the following elements as under: • Climate Change: Carbon Emissions, Product Carbon FootPrints, Financing Environmental Impact, Climate Change Vulnerability • Natural Resources: Water Stress, Bio-diversity & land use, Raw Material sourcing • Pollution & Waste: Toxic emission and waste, Packing Material and waste, E- Waste • Environment Opportunity: Clean Tech, Green Building, Renewable Energy

Ankur, Partner APR & Associates instruct his article assistant CA Mihir to publish a detailed post on its website and social media platforms highlighting its role in conducting “ESG and Digital Audits of a listed manufacturing company using AI-based tools” and to invites other corporates to approach the firm for similar assignments.
Question_Text: Whether the instruction of CA Ankur, Partner of APR & Associates, to his articled assistant CA Mihir is in compliance with the provisions of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 and the Schedules thereto?
Option_A: Yes, since publication of information relating to professional expertise and use of digital audit tools is permitted under the provisions of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 and the Schedules thereto.
Option_B: No, as the instruction results in indirect solicitation of professional work, thereby violating Clause 6 of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949.
Option_C: No, as the instruction results in indirect solicitation of professional work, thereby violating Clause 6 of Part I of the Second Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949.
Option_D: Yes, since ESG assurance and digital auditing are emerging areas and are not restricted provisions of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 and the Schedules thereto.
Reasoning: Clause 6 of Part I of the First Schedule prohibits a Chartered Accountant from advertising or soliciting professional work, directly or indirectly. Publishing posts on websites or social media highlighting assignments and inviting clients amounts to indirect solicitation, even if the client’s name is not disclosed. Therefore, the instruction given by CA Ankur is not in compliance with professional ethics.

Scenario_Text: CA Kishan, a sole proprietor of M/s R K & Co., is a Chartered Accountant in practice. He employs CA Isha, a qualified Chartered Accountant, as a full-time paid assistant. CA Isha conducts audits, prepares reports and signs certificates under the name and supervision of CA Kishan. The firm also engages Mr. Varun, a non-Chartered Accountant, to assist in administrative coordination, client communication and liaison work. The firm pays him a portion of professional receipts described as “legal consultancy and office support charges”, although Mr. Varun does not possess any recognised professional qualification prescribed by the Council. With prior approval of the Council, CA Kishan purchases the goodwill from Ms. Vishakha, wife of a deceased Chartered Accountant. Following this acquisition, the firm witnesses a rapid expansion of its client base, including corporate and overseas clients. During this expansion phase AMC Ltd., one of the client of CA Kishan, had following cyber fraud attempts: • The CFO of a client company receives an urgent phone call from a person impersonating a bank official, requesting OTP details to reverse a supposedly failed transaction. • Around the same time, the Managing Director receives a “Highly Confidential” email appearing to originate from the Chairman, seeking immediate authorisation for an overseas wire transfer. • The attackers leveraged publicly available information and weak segregation of duties to make their communications appear authentic.
Question_Text: Whether payment to Mr. Varun described as “legal consultancy and office support charges” is permissible under the provisions of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 and the Schedules thereto?
Option_A: Permissible sharing of fees due to legal consultancy
Option_B: Professional misconduct under Clause (2) of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949
Option_C: Professional misconduct under Clause (3) of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949
Option_D: Permissible if disclosed in the engagement letter
Reasoning: Clause (2) of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 prohibits a Chartered Accountant in practice from sharing professional fees with a non-member, except in circumstances specifically permitted (such as sharing with members, retired partners, legal representatives of deceased partners, or persons with prescribed qualifications). Merely describing the payment as “legal consultancy” does not alter its substance. Since Mr. Varun is neither a member nor a prescribed professional, sharing fees with him constitutes professional misconduct.

Question_Text: The phone call received by the CFO requesting OTP details to reverse a failed transaction is known as:
Option_A: Whaling
Option_B: Spear phishing
Option_C: Vishing
Option_D: Smishing
Reasoning: Vishing involves fraudulent phone calls impersonating trusted entities such as banks to extract sensitive information like OTPs or passwords. The CFO receiving a call claiming to be from the bank and requesting OTP details clearly fits within vishing attacks.

Question_Text: Whether purchase of goodwill form a non-CA in the given case will tantamount to Professional Misconduct under the provisions of the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 and the Schedules thereto?
Option_A: Purchase of goodwill from a non-CA is professional misconduct under Clause (4) of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949.
Option_B: Purchase of goodwill from the legal heir of a deceased CA, with Council approval, is permissible.
Option_C: Purchase of goodwill is permissible only if the seller is a practicing CA.
Option_D: Purchase of goodwill from a non-CA amount to professional misconduct under Clause (1) of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949
Reasoning: With reference to Clause (2) of Part I of First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949, the Council of the Institute of Chartered Accountants of India considered whether the goodwill of a proprietary concern of chartered accountant can be sold to another member who is otherwise eligible, after the death of the proprietor. It lays down that the sale is permitted subject to certain conditions discussed in the above flowchart. It further resolved that the legal heir of the deceased member has to obtain the permission of the Council within a year of the death of the proprietor concerned.

Question_Text: The email sent to the Managing Director impersonating the Chairman and seeking urgent wire transfer authorisation is an example of:
Option_A: Spear phishing
Option_B: Phishing
Option_C: Whaling
Option_D: Smishing
Reasoning: Whaling is a specialised form of phishing that targets senior executives or decision-makers (such as MDs, CEOs, or Chairpersons) with the objective of authorising high-value transactions. Scenario_Text: M/s BAT & Co., a firm of Chartered Accountants, recently launched its official website “bat-ca.com”. The website featured bright neon colours, details of the partners, and listed some major clients with the fees charged. The website also used a “push” technology, where updates were automatically sent to subscribers without them requesting it. Question_Text: Regarding the website “bat-ca.com”, which of the following statements is correct under the Council General Guidelines, 2008?
Option_A: The firm is fully compliant because there is no restriction on website colours.
Option_B: The firm is in violation of guidelines because it uses “push” technology and discloses client names and fees without regulatory requirement.
Option_C: The website is compliant because disclosure of clients and fees is allowed on the firm’s discretion.
Option_D: The firm is only liable if the website is used to actively solicit new clients.
Reasoning: The Council of the Institute had approved posting of particulars on website by Chartered Accountants in practice under Clause (6) of Part I of First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 subject to the prescribed guidelines. The relevant guidelines in the context of the website hosted by M/s XYZ are: • No restriction on the colours used in the website; • The websites are run on a “pull” technology and not a “push” technology; • Names of clients and fees charged not to be given. However, disclosure of names of clients and/or fees charged, on the website is permissible only where it is required by a regulator, whether or not constituted under a statute, in India or outside India, provided that such disclosure is only to the extent of requirement of the regulator.

Scenario_Text: M/s BAT & Co., a firm of Chartered Accountants, recently launched its official website “bat-ca.com”. The website featured bright neon colours, details of the partners, and listed some major clients with the fees charged. The website also used a “push” technology, where updates were automatically sent to subscribers without them requesting it. Around the same time, Mr. P, a partner in the firm, appeared on a business channel TV interview and handed over a bio-data of the firm to the interviewer. The bio-data contained details of: • The firm’s international affiliations. • Personal achievements and awards of Mr. P as a tax expert. • Notable clients handled by the firm. Later, another partner, Mr. Q, published a congratulatory advertisement in a leading newspaper with his photograph on the occasion of opening a new branch office. Further, M/s BAT & Co. was appointed as the statutory auditor of RST Ltd., a company covered under CARO 2020. While reporting under Paragraph 3 of CARO 2020 relating to property, plant and equipment, inventory, statutory dues, and borrowings, the audit team relied on system-generated MIS reports from the company’s ERP system, and management representations confirming the accuracy and completeness of such information. The audit team did not carry out independent verification of underlying records or evaluate the effectiveness of internal controls, on the assumption that consistency between MIS reports and management representations provides sufficient audit evidence.
Question_Text: In the given case, whether approach of audit team i.e., relying on written representation and MIS with respect to reporting under relevant Standards on Auditing and CARO 2020 is appropriate?
Option_A: The auditor’s approach is appropriate since consistency between MIS reports and management representations is sufficient for compliance with SAs and CARO 2020 reporting.
Option_B: The auditor’s approach is acceptable as reporting largely involves matters based on information and explanations provided by management during the audit.
Option_C: The auditor’s approach is inappropriate because management representations and system-generated MIS cannot substitute independent audit procedures required for compliance with SAs and CARO 2020 reporting.
Option_D: The auditor’s approach is justified unless there is a specific indication of fraud or material misstatement.
Reasoning: CARO 2020 reporting is an integral part of the statutory audit and requires the auditor to obtain sufficient and appropriate audit evidence. Further, as per SA 580, “Written Representations”, management representations serve only as supplementary audit evidence, and cannot replace independent verification of records, evaluation of internal controls, and other audit procedures. Accordingly, reliance solely on MIS reports and representations—without independent verification—is not adequate for reporting and reflects non-compliance.

Question_Text: Whether Mr. P will be held guilty for professional misconduct for handing over the firm’s bio-data during a TV interview under the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 and Schedules thereto?
Option_A: Yes, Mr. P will be held guilty of professional misconduct under Clause (6) of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 because handing over bio-data promoting the firm and the partner’s achievements indirectly solicits professional work.
Option_B: No, Mr. P will not be held guilty of professional misconduct as the partner did not personally read out the bio-data on TV.
Option_C: No, Mr. P will not be held guilty of professional misconduct as long as the bio-data was factual.
Option_D: Yes, Mr. P will be held guilty of professional misconduct under Clause (6) of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 only if the TV channel ran advertisements alongside the bio-data.
Reasoning: Clause (6) of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 prohibits solicitation of client or professional work either directly or indirectly by circular, advertisement, personal communication or interview or by any other means since it shall constitute professional misconduct.

Question_Text: Whether the publishing of the photograph by Mr. Q is permissible under the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949 and the Schedules thereto?
Option_A: Yes, it is permissible as it merely conveys information about an event and does not amount to solicitation of professional work.
Option_B: Yes, it is permissible since the publication highlights personal credentials of the partner and does not involve advertisement of professional services.
Option_C: No, it is not permissible as it indirectly solicits professional work and promotes the partner and the firm and tantamount to professional misconduct.
Option_D: Yes, it is permissible provided the publication does not contain any reference to services rendered or fees charged.
Reasoning: As per Clause (6) of Part I of the First Schedule to the Chartered Accountants Act, 1949, a Chartered Accountant in practice shall be deemed to be guilty of misconduct if he solicits clients or professional work either directly or indirectly by a circular, advertisement, personal communication or interview or by any other means.

Scenario_Text: Verma & Co. has been appointed as the central statutory auditor of Quick Trust Bank Ltd., a private sector bank registered with RBI, for F.Y. 2023–24. Mr. Verma, the engagement partner, while examining the computation of DTL (Demand and Time Liabilities), raised his suspicion regarding the inclusion and exclusion of the following items: Sr. No. Particulars of Item Inclusion/Exclusion 1 Amounts received in INR for disposal of import bills shown under Sundry Deposits Inclusion 2 Net credit balance of Branch Adjustment Account Inclusion 3 Amount received from ECGC by invoking the guarantee Exclusion 4 Margins held and kept in sundry deposits for funded facilities. Inclusion 5 Unaccounted inward remittances received on behalf of customers Inclusion During the course of audit, Verma & Co. also observed that one large advance account of the bank had no recovery of principal or interest for the past 18 months. The bank has not applied the NPA norms as well as income recognition norms to this particular account. When queried the bank management replied that this account was guaranteed by the central government and hence these norms were not applicable. The bank has not invoked the guarantee. Additionally, Verma & Co. was offered Stock audit of a sister concern of Quick Trust Bank Ltd. for F.Y. 2023–24, and Branch audit of its Andheri Branch for F.Y. 2023–24 and Bandra Branch for F.Y. 2024–25, respectively.
Question_Text: Items at which serial number, as aforesaid, have been wrongly included or excluded for the computation of Demand and Time Liabilities?
Option_A: Items at which Sr. No. 1 & 4, respectively.
Option_B: Items at which Sr. No. 2, 3 & 5, respectively.
Option_C: Items at which Sr. No. 1 & 3, respectively.
Option_D: Items at which Sr. No. 3 & 5, respectively.
Reasoning: While examining the computation of DTL, specifically examine that following items have been excluded from liabilities: • Amounts received in Indian currency against import bills and held in sundry deposits pending receipts of final rates • Amount received from ECGC by invoking the guarantee • Margins held and kept in sundry deposits for funded facilities. Similarly, specifically examine that the following items have been included in liabilities- • Net credit balance in branch adjustment accounts including these relating to foreign branches. • The reconciliation of Nostro accounts (with Nostro Mirror Accounts) needs to be scrutinized carefully to analyze and ascertain if any inwards remittances are received on behalf of the customers / constituents of the bank and have remained unaccounted and / or any other debit (inward) entries have remained unaccounted and are pertaining to any liabilities for the bank. Scenario_Text: Verma & Co. has been appointed as the central statutory auditor of Quick Trust Bank Ltd., a private sector bank registered with RBI, for F.Y. 2023–24. Mr. Verma, the engagement partner, while examining the computation of DTL (Demand and Time Liabilities), raised his suspicion regarding the inclusion and exclusion of the following items: Sr. No. Particulars of Item Inclusion/Exclusion 1 Amounts received in INR for disposal of import bills shown under Sundry Deposits Inclusion 2 Net credit balance of Branch Adjustment Account Inclusion 3 Amount received from ECGC by invoking the guarantee Exclusion 4 Margins held and kept in sundry deposits for funded facilities. Inclusion 5 Unaccounted inward remittances received on behalf of customers Inclusion During the course of audit, Verma & Co. also observed that one large advance account of the bank had no recovery of principal or interest for the past 18 months. The bank has not applied the NPA norms as well as income recognition norms to this particular account. When queried the bank management replied that this account was guaranteed by the central government and hence these norms were not applicable. The bank has not invoked the guarantee. Additionally, Verma & Co. was offered Stock audit of a sister concern of Quick Trust Bank Ltd. for F.Y. 2023–24, and Branch audit of its Andheri Branch for F.Y. 2023–24 and Bandra Branch for F.Y. 2024–25, respectively.
Question_Text: Whether the classification of advance and income recognition by Quick Trust Bank Ltd. is correct?
Option_A: The bank is incorrect in not classifying the advance as NPA and in continuing to recognize interest income, since guarantees do not affect IRAC norms.
Option_B: The bank is correct in not classifying the advance as NPA for provisioning purposes since the Central Government guarantee has not been repudiated, but interest income should not be recognized unless actually realized.
Option_C: The bank is correct in both not classifying the advance as NPA and in recognizing interest income on accrual basis due to Central Government guarantee.
Option_D: The bank should classify the advance as NPA only after 90 days of overdue irrespective of whether the guarantee is by Central or State Government.
Reasoning: If a government guaranteed advance becomes NPA, then for the purpose of income recognition, interest on such advance should not be taken to income unless interest is realized. However, for the purpose of asset classification, credit facility backed by Central Government Guarantee, though overdue, can be treated as NPA only when the Central Government repudiates its guarantee, when invoked. Since the bank has not invoked the guarantee, the question of repudiation does not arise. Hence the bank is correct to the extent of not applying the NPA norms for provisioning purpose. But this exemption is not available in respect of income recognition norms. Hence the income to the extent not recovered should be reversed.

Mr. Verma, the engagement partner, while examining the computation of DTL (Demand and Time Liabilities), raised his suspicion regarding the inclusion and exclusion of the following items: Sr. No. Particulars of Item Inclusion/Exclusion 1 Amounts received in INR for disposal of import bills shown under Sundry Deposits Inclusion 2 Net credit balance of Branch Adjustment Account Inclusion 3 Amount received from ECGC by invoking the guarantee Exclusion 4 Margins held and kept in sundry deposits for funded facilities. Inclusion 5 Unaccounted inward remittances received on behalf of customers Inclusion During the course of audit, Verma & Co. also observed that one large advance account of the bank had no recovery of principal or interest for the past 18 months. The bank has not applied the NPA norms as well as income recognition norms to this particular account. When queried the bank management replied that this account was guaranteed by the central government and hence these norms were not applicable. The bank has not invoked the guarantee. Additionally, Verma & Co. was offered Stock audit of a sister concern of Quick Trust Bank Ltd. for F.Y. 2023–24, and Branch audit of its Andheri Branch for F.Y. 2023–24 and Bandra Branch for F.Y. 2024–25, respectively.
Question_Text: Would the auditor’s conclusion change if the advance, which has remained overdue for more than 18 months, was guaranteed by a State Government instead of the Central Government?
Option_A: Yes, since the special exemption from NPA classification is not available for State Government guaranteed advances, and the account would be treated as NPA after 90 days of overdue.
Option_B: Yes, but only income recognition norms would change, while asset classification would remain unaffected.
Option_C: No, both Central and State Government guarantees are treated identically for asset classification and income recognition under RBI IRAC norms.
Option_D: No, the account would be classified as NPA only on invocation and repudiation of the State Government guarantee.
Reasoning: As per RBI IRAC norms, the exemption from asset classification applicable to Central Government guaranteed advances is not available in case of State Government guaranteed advances. Accordingly, if an advance guaranteed by a State Government remains overdue for more than 90 days, it is required to be classified as NPA, irrespective of invocation of the guarantee.

Scenario_Text: Verma & Co. has been appointed as the central statutory auditor of Quick Trust Bank Ltd., a private sector bank registered with RBI, for F.Y. 2023–24. Mr. Verma, the engagement partner, while examining the computation of DTL (Demand and Time Liabilities), raised his suspicion regarding the inclusion and exclusion of the following items: Sr. No. Particulars of Item Inclusion/Exclusion 1 Amounts received in INR for disposal of import bills shown under Sundry Deposits Inclusion 2 Net credit balance of Branch Adjustment Account Inclusion 3 Amount received from ECGC by invoking the guarantee Exclusion 4 Margins held and kept in sundry deposits for funded facilities. Inclusion 5 Unaccounted inward remittances received on behalf of customers Inclusion During the course of audit, Verma & Co. also observed that one large advance account of the bank had no recovery of principal or interest for the past 18 months. The bank has not applied the NPA norms as well as income recognition norms to this particular account. When queried the bank management replied that this account was guaranteed by the central government and hence these norms were not applicable. The bank has not invoked the guarantee. Additionally, Verma & Co. was offered Stock audit of a sister concern of Quick Trust Bank Ltd. for F.Y. 2023–24, and Branch audit of its Andheri Branch for F.Y. 2023–24 and Bandra Branch for F.Y. 2024–25, respectively.
Question_Text: Whether Verma & Co. can accept the stock audit assignments, as aforesaid?
Option_A: It can only accept the stock audit assignment of its Bandra Branch and its Andheri Branch.
Option_B: It can only accept the stock audit assignment of its Bandra Branch.
Option_C: It can only accept the stock audit assignment of the sister concern of Quick Trust Bank Ltd., its Bandra Branch as well as its Andheri Branch. However, it will require NOC from Quick Trust Bank Ltd. for accepting the stock audit assignment of its sister concern.
Option_D: It can only accept the stock audit assignment of the sister concern of Quick Trust Bank Ltd.
Reasoning: As per Recent Decisions of Ethical Standards Board a chartered accountant who is the statutory auditor of a bank cannot for the same financial year accept stock audit of the same branch of the bank or any of the branches of the same bank or sister concern of the bank, for the same financial year. In the present case, Verma & Co. is the statutory auditor of Quick Trust Bank Ltd. for F.Y. 2022–23. Accordingly, it cannot accept the stock audit assignment of the sister concern of Quick Trust Bank Ltd. for F.Y. 2022–23, nor can it accept the stock audit of its Andheri Branch for F.Y. 2022–23, as both pertain to the same financial year. However, the stock audit assignment of the Bandra Branch for F.Y. 2023–24 relates to a different financial year. Hence, the restriction does not apply, and Verma & Co. can accept only the stock audit of the Bandra Branch for F.Y. 2023–24.
